_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology from Meier & Kagan 2019. Pathophysiology cross-referenced with Brook I 2009 PMID 19272086 (suppurative lymphadenitis), Rajasekaran & Bhatt 2023 PMID 36088677. Antibiotic evidence from IDSA skin structure infection guidelines 2014. Pediatric cervical lymphadenitis data from Swanson 2017 PMID 28780871.

h1Title: Acute Lymphadenitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Acute lymphadenitis is a rapid inflammatory enlargement of one or more lymph nodes caused by bacterial, viral, or, less commonly, fungal infection draining from an adjacent or distant focus. It represents the most common reason for a tender neck, axillary, or inguinal lump in both children and adults; in children under 10, group A Streptococcus and Staphylococcus aureus account for approximately 70-80% of unilateral bacterial cases. Most episodes resolve within 2-4 weeks with targeted antibiotic therapy, but delayed treatment can lead to lymph-node abscess requiring surgical drainage. A key clinical priority is distinguishing reactive lymphadenitis — which dominates in the outpatient setting — from lymphoma, tuberculosis, and atypical mycobacterial infection.

keyStats.prevalence: Exact population prevalence is not precisely established; lymph node enlargement is documented in up to 38-44% of healthy children on examination; bacterial lymphadenitis accounts for an estimated 1-2 per 100 primary care visits (AAP 2021)
keyStats.demographics: Children aged 1-10 years bear the heaviest burden of bacterial cervical lymphadenitis; adults over 50 with persistent lymphadenopathy require more aggressive evaluation to exclude malignancy
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence of acute bacterial cervical lymphadenitis at age 2-5 years; all ages susceptible depending on source infection
keyStats.globalCases: No reliable global incidence figure; cervical lymphadenitis accounts for 20-40% of referrals to pediatric neck surgeons in high-income settings

definition:
Acute lymphadenitis (ICD-10: L04.9) is an acute inflammatory condition of a lymph node or lymph-node group, characterized by rapid enlargement, tenderness, erythema of overlying skin, and warmth, resulting from the influx of inflammatory cells and expansion of germinal centers in response to an infective antigen. The condition differs from reactive lymphadenopathy — the generalized, painless node enlargement seen in viral upper respiratory infections — by its localized nature, tenderness, and the presence of a definable infectious focus.

Pathophysiologically, microorganisms or their antigens are transported from a primary site of infection (skin wound, dental infection, tonsillar abscess, scalp furuncle, or genital ulcer) via afferent lymphatics to the draining lymph node, where macrophage activation, neutrophil infiltration, and lymphocyte proliferation produce the characteristic enlargement and pain. When the infective load overwhelms local defenses, liquefaction necrosis occurs, converting lymphadenitis into a fluctuant suppurative abscess requiring drainage.

Bacterial causes predominate in clinical practice: Staphylococcus aureus and group A Streptococcus pyogenes together account for 65-80% of unilateral bacterial cervical lymphadenitis. Staphylococcus aureus, including community-acquired methicillin-resistant strains (CA-MRSA), is the most common single pathogen in purulent presentations. Management spans primary care to surgery; infective disease, dermatology, head and neck surgery, and pediatrics are the most commonly involved specialties.

typesClassification.description: Early-stage acute lymphadenitis with a firm or rubbery, tender, erythematous node without fluctuance. Responsive to oral antibiotics targeting Staphylococcus and Streptococcus; most cases in this category resolve without abscess formation within 7-14 days of treatment.

Advanced stage with central liquefaction necrosis; the overlying skin becomes erythematous and thinned, and fluctuance is detectable on palpation. Often caused by S. aureus, including MRSA. Requires incision and drainage or needle aspiration plus antibiotics.

Bilateral, non-tender or mildly tender cervical or axillary lymph node enlargement in the context of a viral illness — Epstein-Barr virus, cytomegalovirus, adenovirus, or SARS-CoV-2. Typically self-limiting over 2-6 weeks without antibiotic therapy.

Caused by non-tuberculous mycobacteria (especially M. avium complex) in children under 5 years; presents as a violaceous, slowly enlarging, non-tender submandibular or preauricular node that may spontaneously drain. Does not respond to standard antibiotics; managed with excision or macrolide-based regimens.

Prolonged (over 4 weeks) suppurative lymphadenitis, often with sinus tract formation. Causes include Actinomyces, Bartonella henselae (cat-scratch disease), and partially treated bacterial lymphadenitis. Warrants biopsy to exclude tuberculosis or lymphoma.

primarySymptoms: Rapid enlargement of a lymph node group — typically cervical, axillary, or inguinal — reaching 1-3 cm or larger within 24-48 hours of onset, accompanied by localized pain that worsens with pressure or movement.
Overlying skin erythema, warmth, and tenderness indicating extension of inflammation beyond the lymph-node capsule into the surrounding tissue.
Fever above 38°C (100.4°F) in the majority of bacterial cases, often with chills, malaise, and fatigue in proportion to the severity of the primary infection.
Fluctuance — a soft, wave-like sensation on palpation — indicating liquefaction necrosis and abscess formation within the node, found in approximately 25-30% of untreated bacterial cases.
Loss of the normal discrete, mobile quality of the lymph node; the node becomes fixed, tender, and may coalesce with adjacent nodes to form a matted mass.

An identifiable primary infection focus in the drainage territory — scalp folliculitis, facial impetigo, dental abscess, tonsillitis, hand wound, or genital herpes — serving as the entry point for the causative organism.
Dysphagia, trismus, or stiff neck when severe cervical lymphadenitis affects the anterior or posterior triangle, compressing adjacent structures.
Spontaneous rupture of an abscess through the overlying skin with purulent discharge, producing a sinus tract if the underlying node is not adequately treated.
Systemic signs of sepsis — tachycardia, hypotension, altered mental status — in neglected cases where infection has spread beyond the node into surrounding soft tissue or the bloodstream.
earlyWarningSigns: A single, tender, firm lymph node appearing within 24-48 hours of a skin infection, dental procedure, or sore throat in the same drainage region — the earliest sign before fluctuance or erythema develops.
Progressive increase in node size despite 48-72 hours of empirical oral antibiotics, signaling either abscess formation or an atypical organism requiring different management.
Skin over a lymph node that becomes shiny, thinned, and erythematous — a sign of impending spontaneous rupture if drainage is not performed.
New fever spike in a child with known tonsillitis or skin infection suggesting secondary lymph-node involvement.
emergencySigns: Fever above 39°C with a rapidly expanding neck mass, trismus, drooling, or difficulty breathing — features suggesting parapharyngeal or retropharyngeal abscess requiring emergency airway management and surgical drainage.

Signs of sepsis (heart rate over 120 bpm, confusion, systolic blood pressure below 90 mmHg) originating from a suppurative lymph node — indicates bacteremia requiring intravenous antibiotics and hospital admission.
New stridor, hoarseness, or inspiratory difficulty associated with large cervical lymphadenitis — compressive airway emergency requiring immediate ENT evaluation.
Rapidly spreading erythema beyond the node consistent with necrotizing fasciitis — surgical emergency with mortality above 25% without timely debridement.

causes.description: The most common cause of purulent unilateral cervical, axillary, and inguinal lymphadenitis in children and adults. S. aureus produces toxins including panton-valentine leukocidin in CA-MRSA strains that promote rapid abscess formation. Source is typically a skin or soft tissue infection (furuncle, impetigo, wound) in the draining territory.

The second most common bacterial cause, often arising from tonsillitis, pharyngitis, or skin infection. Streptococcal lymphadenitis tends to present as firm, extremely tender, erythematous nodes rather than fluctuant abscesses; bacteremia and rheumatic fever are rare complications.

Adenovirus, rhinovirus, EBV, and CMV trigger reactive bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy through lymphocyte expansion and macrophage activation in the cervical nodes. Viral lymphadenitis is typically bilateral, less tender than bacterial cases, and resolves spontaneously over 2-6 weeks.

The leading cause of chronic cervical lymphadenitis in children under 5 in high-income countries. MAC is acquired from soil or water, not person-to-person. The node enlarges slowly, becomes violaceous and fluctuant, and does not respond to standard antibiotics.

Odontogenic infection — including periapical abscesses, periodontitis, and post-extraction infection — drains to the submandibular and anterior cervical nodes. Mixed oral anaerobes (Fusobacterium, Prevotella) and streptococci are the usual pathogens; these require dental source control alongside antibiotic therapy.

Cat scratch disease is transmitted through the scratch or bite of a bacteremic kitten and causes a primary papule at the inoculation site followed in 1-3 weeks by regional lymphadenopathy — most often axillary, cervical, or epitrochlear — that is tender and may suppurate. Self-limiting in immunocompetent individuals over 2-4 months.

riskFactors.description: Young children have immature immune systems and encounter endemic organisms (group A strep, S. aureus) through skin breaks and tonsillitis at higher rates. Cervical lymphadenitis is the dominant pediatric presentation.

riskFactors.factor: Active skin or soft tissue infection in the drainage territory
riskFactors.description: An untreated scalp furuncle, impetigo, hand wound, or dental abscess adjacent to a lymph-node group is the most immediate risk factor; treating the primary source reduces lymphadenitis risk.

HIV infection, chemotherapy, organ transplantation, or systemic corticosteroids impair lymphocyte and neutrophil responses, predisposing to more severe, poorly localized lymphadenitis and atypical organisms including fungi and MAC.
Bartonella henselae, the agent of cat-scratch disease, is transmitted by kitten scratches or bites in approximately 90% of cases. Children are more likely to play roughly with kittens.

riskFactors.factor: Poor dentition or recent dental procedure
riskFactors.description: Untreated caries, periapical abscesses, and post-extraction dry socket seed oral streptococci and anaerobes into submandibular nodes. Regular dental care directly reduces this pathway.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or travel to tuberculosis-endemic regions
riskFactors.description: Mycobacterium tuberculosis causes cervical scrofula — painless, matted, often bilateral node enlargement — in up to 5% of adults with pulmonary TB in high-burden countries. Travel history is a critical diagnostic variable.

affectedDemographics: Children aged 1-10: the most heavily affected group for acute bacterial cervical lymphadenitis, with S. aureus and group A strep the dominant pathogens.
Young adults aged 15-30: high rates of EBV mononucleosis-associated posterior cervical lymphadenitis; also at risk for cat-scratch disease axillary lymphadenitis.
Adults over 50 with persistent lymphadenopathy: elevated risk that the etiology is lymphoma or metastatic cancer rather than infection, warranting systematic evaluation.
Immunocompromised patients (HIV, transplant, chemotherapy): susceptible to atypical presentations with MAC, Histoplasma, Cryptococcus, or disseminated bacterial infection.
diagnosisOverview:
Acute lymphadenitis is primarily a clinical diagnosis based on history, physical examination, and identification of a primary infection focus. The clinical approach follows a sequence: history of primary infection or exposure, node characteristics (site, size, consistency, mobility, tenderness, skin changes), and systemic signs of infection.

Bacterial lymphadenitis typically produces a unilateral, warm, tender, erythematous node in the draining territory of a known infection. Blood tests — full blood count, CRP, ESR — support the diagnosis by showing leukocytosis with a neutrophil predominance and elevated acute-phase reactants. Blood cultures are drawn in febrile patients to identify bacteremia.

Ultrasound is the first-line imaging investigation, distinguishing a reactive enlarged node (normal architecture, no central necrosis) from an abscess (loss of central echogenicity, fluid rim, liquefaction). CT scan of the neck with contrast is used when deep-space infection, retropharyngeal abscess, or vascular involvement is suspected.

Biopsy — fine-needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) or core-needle biopsy — is reserved for nodes that persist beyond 4-6 weeks despite antibiotic treatment, nodes over 3 cm without tenderness or warmth, or cases with systemic features suggesting lymphoma (night sweats, weight loss, painless rubbery nodes). FNAC yields microbiological specimens for culture, Mycobacterium tuberculosis PCR, and cytology in the same pass.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count and C-reactive protein
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms systemic inflammation, quantifies leukocyte count, and distinguishes bacterial (neutrophilia, high CRP) from viral (lymphocytosis, low CRP) causes
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood draw from an arm vein. Results available within 2-4 hours in most hospitals. Bacterial lymphadenitis typically shows white cell count over 12,000/µL with more than 70% neutrophils.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies abscess formation (loss of echogenicity, necrotic center), guides aspiration, and measures node size for follow-up
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A cool gel is applied to the skin and a probe is moved across the affected area. No pain, no radiation. Takes 10-15 minutes. Shows whether the node contains pus — a finding that changes management from oral antibiotics to drainage.

diagnosticTests.test: Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) with Gram stain and culture
diagnosticTests.purpose: Obtains microbiological specimens from a suppurative node for organism identification and antibiotic susceptibility testing
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: After skin cleaning and a small local anesthetic injection, a fine needle is passed into the node and a small amount of fluid or cells is aspirated. Mild discomfort. Results of Gram stain in 24 hours; culture in 2-5 days. Identifies MRSA, streptococci, and atypical organisms.

diagnosticTests.test: CT scan of the neck or affected region with contrast
diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates extent of infection, deep-space extension, vascular involvement, and parapharyngeal or retropharyngeal abscess in severe cervical cases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Lie still for 5-10 minutes while a doughnut-shaped scanner takes images. Contrast dye is injected into a vein. Mild flushing sensation is common. Results reviewed by radiologist, usually within 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms cat-scratch disease in patients with axillary or cervical lymphadenopathy and cat or kitten contact in the past 3-6 weeks
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw. IgM rises 3-4 weeks after infection; IgG peaks at 6-8 weeks. Results in 3-7 days. A fourfold rise in paired sera or a single high IgG titer confirms diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.test: Mycobacterial culture, PCR, and QuantiFERON-TB Gold
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes Mycobacterium tuberculosis and non-tuberculous mycobacterial infection in persistent or atypical lymphadenitis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood is taken for the interferon-gamma release assay (QuantiFERON); if FNAC was performed, the aspirate is submitted for acid-fast smear, mycobacterial culture (6-8 weeks to finalize), and PCR (5-10 days). Tuberculin skin test is an alternative to QuantiFERON.

treatmentOverview:
Treatment of acute lymphadenitis is guided by clinical presentation (suppurative vs non-suppurative), severity, likely pathogen, and antimicrobial susceptibility data in the local area. IDSA skin and soft tissue infection guidelines (Stevens et al. 2014) and AAP Red Book (2021) are the principal clinical references.

For non-suppurative bacterial lymphadenitis, oral antibiotics targeting S. aureus and Streptococcus are first-line: cefalexin 500 mg four times daily (25-50 mg/kg/day in children) or amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily for 10-14 days. In communities with high CA-MRSA rates (generally above 20% prevalence in wound isolates), trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) or clindamycin are preferred empirical agents pending culture results.

Suppurative lymphadenitis with abscess formation requires incision and drainage or ultrasound-guided needle aspiration as the primary intervention, combined with systemic antibiotics. Aspirated material is sent for Gram stain, aerobic and anaerobic culture, and in appropriate epidemiological contexts, mycobacterial studies.

Intravenous antibiotics (vancomycin 25-30 mg/kg/day in divided doses for MRSA; nafcillin or oxacillin for methicillin-susceptible S. aureus; penicillin G for streptococcal bacteremia) are used for patients with systemic sepsis, inability to tolerate oral medication, failure of outpatient therapy, or nodes in anatomically dangerous locations (carotid space, mediastinum, retropharynx).

Viral lymphadenitis requires only supportive care — rest, analgesics, and hydration. Cat-scratch disease is usually self-limiting; azithromycin 500 mg on day 1, then 250 mg/day for 4 days modestly accelerates resolution in severe or immunocompromised cases. Atypical mycobacterial lymphadenitis is treated with surgical excision (curative in 90%) or macrolide-based regimens (clarithromycin or azithromycin, sometimes with rifampicin) for inoperable or recurrent disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Cefalexin (500 mg four times daily, 10-14 days)
medicalTreatments.description: First-generation cephalosporin; first-line for non-suppurative bacterial lymphadenitis where CA-MRSA prevalence is below 20%. Active against S. aureus (methicillin-susceptible) and Streptococcus pyogenes. Well-tolerated in children at 25-50 mg/kg/day in divided doses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolution in 80-90% of non-MRSA non-suppurative cases when the primary infection focus is controlled (Stevens et al. IDSA 2014)

medicalTreatments.name: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 1-2 DS tablets twice daily (10-14 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Preferred empirical oral agent for suppurative lymphadenitis in areas with CA-MRSA prevalence above 20%. Active against most CA-MRSA strains. Avoid in sulfa allergy, renal impairment, or G6PD deficiency.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure rates 80-90% in outpatient CA-MRSA skin and soft tissue infections in US studies; no randomized trial specific to lymphadenitis

medicalTreatments.name: Clindamycin (300-450 mg three times daily, 10-14 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative agent for CA-MRSA, MSSA, and oral streptococcal infections; active against anaerobes, making it suitable for odontogenic lymphadenitis. Check D-zone testing in CA-MRSA for inducible resistance before use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable to TMP-SMX for outpatient CA-MRSA infections in RCTs; active against anaerobes in dental-origin cases

medicalTreatments.name: Vancomycin IV (25-30 mg/kg/day in 2-3 divided doses)
medicalTreatments.description: Intravenous glycopeptide for MRSA bacteremia, sepsis, or failure of oral therapy. Target AUC/MIC-guided dosing increasingly preferred over trough-only monitoring. Continue until clinically improved and patient can tolerate oral step-down.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Standard of care for MRSA bacteremia; 80-90% clinical success rates in skin and soft tissue infection meta-analyses

medicalTreatments.name: Azithromycin (day 1: 500 mg; days 2-5: 250 mg) for cat-scratch disease
medicalTreatments.description: Macrolide antibiotic that reduces node size by 80% at day 30 versus placebo in randomized trial (Bass et al. 1998). Used for moderate-to-severe Bartonella henselae lymphadenitis; immunocompromised patients need longer courses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 50% reduction in lymph node volume at 30 days vs placebo in the only RCT; self-limiting disease in immunocompetent patients often resolves without treatment

medicalTreatments.name: Amoxicillin-clavulanate (875/125 mg twice daily, 10-14 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combination covering MSSA, Streptococcus, and oral anaerobes. Good choice for odontogenic lymphadenitis or mixed-flora wound-origin cases. Avoid in known MRSA or true penicillin allergy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical resolution in 85-90% of MSSA and streptococcal lymphadenitis when the primary source is treated concurrently

surgicalOptions.name: Incision and drainage
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical incision of the overlying skin under local or general anesthetic, evacuation of pus, and placement of a wick or drain to prevent re-accumulation. Standard treatment for fluctuant lymph-node abscesses over 2-3 cm. Performed in the emergency department or operating theater depending on depth and anatomy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Clinical resolution in 90-95% of superficial abscesses; recurrence 5-10% if antibiotics are not continued post-drainage (Loop RCT 2017)

surgicalOptions.name: Ultrasound-guided needle aspiration
surgicalOptions.description: Ultrasound-guided aspiration with an 18-gauge needle under local anesthetic; less morbid than incision for nodes that are accessible but not yet frankly fluctuant. Yields material for culture. May require repeat aspiration in 20-30% of cases.
surgicalOptions.successRate: First-attempt cure rate approximately 70%; useful in children to avoid general anesthetic for smaller abscesses

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical excision (atypical mycobacterial lymphadenitis)
surgicalOptions.description: Complete surgical excision of MAC-infected lymph nodes under general anesthetic is curative in up to 90% of children with atypical mycobacterial cervical lymphadenitis; preferred over medical therapy because MAC is intrinsically resistant to most standard antibiotics.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 90% cure rate with complete excision; incomplete excision carries 25-35% recurrence or sinus tract formation rate

whySeeSpecialist: A general practitioner can manage most cases of acute bacterial lymphadenitis empirically. Specialist referral to an infectious disease physician, ENT surgeon, or oral and maxillofacial surgeon is warranted when lymphadenitis has not responded to 10-14 days of appropriate antibiotics, when deep cervical or mediastinal extension is suspected, or when abscess anatomy precludes safe office drainage. Persistent lymphadenopathy beyond 6 weeks always warrants oncology or hematology evaluation to exclude lymphoma.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a clinician who will culture aspirated material and adjust antibiotics based on sensitivity data rather than continuing empirical therapy when there is inadequate improvement. For children with suspected atypical mycobacterial or persistent suppurative lymphadenitis, a pediatric infectious disease specialist in combination with a pediatric head and neck or general surgeon provides optimal care.
hospitalCriteria: Systemic sepsis with suspected lymph-node origin requiring intravenous antibiotics
Deep cervical lymphadenitis with potential for airway compromise
Failure of 72-hour outpatient oral antibiotic therapy with progressive node enlargement or increasing fever
Immunocompromised patients with febrile lymphadenitis requiring intravenous therapy and close monitoring
keyFacilities: Emergency departments for sepsis management and urgent drainage
ENT and oral-maxillofacial surgery units for deep cervical cases
Pediatric infectious disease clinics for complex or recurrent cases
Histopathology and microbiology laboratories for culture and biopsy
preventionStrategies: Treat skin infections, wounds, dental abscesses, and tonsillitis promptly with appropriate antibiotics to prevent bacterial seeding of draining lymph nodes.
Clean and cover all skin wounds, abrasions, and animal scratch injuries with antiseptic solution and a sterile dressing; replace daily until healed.
Maintain regular dental hygiene — twice-daily brushing, flossing, and 6-monthly dental review — to prevent periodontal infection from seeding submandibular nodes.
Supervise young children around kittens to minimize scratch and bite injuries and reduce Bartonella henselae transmission risk.
Maintain up-to-date vaccinations, including those for Haemophilus influenzae type b, to prevent bacteremic infections that can cause secondary lymphadenitis.
lifestyleModifications: Complete the full antibiotic course even if symptoms improve within 3-4 days, to prevent partial treatment of the lymph-node infection and abscess formation.
Apply warm compresses to the affected lymph node 3-4 times daily to promote drainage and reduce discomfort; do not attempt to drain the node at home.
Rest and limit physical activity during febrile illness; lymph-node tissue is tender and local trauma from exercise can worsen inflammation.
Treat any identified primary infection focus — dental caries, skin folliculitis, tonsil abscess — alongside systemic antibiotics to prevent reseeding the lymph node.
Return to care within 48-72 hours if fever worsens, the node enlarges further, or overlying skin becomes fluctuant or breaks down despite starting antibiotics.

recommended: Adequate hydration (at least 2 L/day) to support immune function and antibiotic distribution
High-protein foods (lean meat, eggs, legumes) to support immune cell production and tissue repair
Vitamin C-rich foods (citrus, kiwi, peppers) to support leukocyte function during acute infection
Iron-rich foods if concurrent anemia from chronic or recurrent infection
avoid: Alcohol during antibiotic courses, particularly metronidazole, clindamycin, and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, which have adverse interactions
Dairy products within 2 hours of tetracycline-class antibiotics, which impair absorption

exerciseGuidelines: Rest is recommended during the acute febrile phase. Light activity such as walking can resume once fever has resolved and pain is controlled. Strenuous exercise and contact sports should be avoided until lymph node tenderness and swelling have fully resolved, typically 2-4 weeks, to prevent trauma to the inflamed node.
dailyManagement: Take antibiotics at evenly spaced intervals and with food where tolerated to maximize blood levels and minimize gastrointestinal side effects.
Apply warm moist compresses to the affected area for 15-20 minutes three to four times daily to reduce discomfort and promote local circulation.
Monitor node size and skin changes daily; photograph the area with a ruler for objective tracking.
Attend scheduled 48-72-hour follow-up with the treating clinician to confirm adequate response or escalate treatment.
Keep primary infection sites (skin wounds, mouth) clean and covered to prevent re-inoculation of draining lymph nodes.
prognosis: Acute bacterial lymphadenitis carries an excellent prognosis when diagnosed and treated promptly. Non-suppurative cases treated with appropriate oral antibiotics resolve in 7-14 days in more than 85% of patients. Suppurative cases requiring drainage typically resolve within 2-4 weeks post-drainage with adjuvant antibiotics, leaving a small residual fibrous nodule that gradually diminishes over months. CA-MRSA lymphadenitis has higher recurrence rates (approximately 10-15% within 6 months) if underlying skin colonization is not addressed with decolonization protocols (intranasal mupirocin and chlorhexidine washes). Atypical mycobacterial lymphadenitis treated with complete excision resolves in over 90% of cases. Cat-scratch disease is self-limiting in immunocompetent individuals over 2-4 months, even without antibiotics. Complications including bacteremia, deep-space infection, and jugular-vein thrombosis are rare (under 1%) in treated patients but substantially increase morbidity and length of stay when they occur.
recoveryTimeline: Non-suppurative bacterial lymphadenitis: fever resolves within 48-72 hours of antibiotics; node tenderness improves within 5-7 days; full resolution by 2-4 weeks. Suppurative lymphadenitis after drainage: fever clears within 24-48 hours; wound heals in 7-14 days; residual nodule may persist for 4-12 weeks. Cat-scratch disease: spontaneous resolution over 2-4 months in most immunocompetent patients.
complications: Abscess formation — untreated or inadequately treated bacterial lymphadenitis liquefies to an abscess requiring surgical drainage in approximately 25-30% of cases; delay increases wound size and healing time.
Bacteremia and sepsis — staphylococcal or streptococcal lymphadenitis can seed the bloodstream; recognized early by fever spike, rigors, and tachycardia; mandates blood cultures and IV antibiotics.
Deep-space neck infection — cervical lymphadenitis can extend into the parapharyngeal, retropharyngeal, or danger spaces with risk of mediastinitis; CT with contrast identifies this complication.

Lemierre syndrome — rare but life-threatening thrombophlebitis of the internal jugular vein following pharyngeal infection and posterior cervical lymphadenitis; presents with septic emboli to the lungs.
Sinus tract formation — inadequately treated or MAC-infected nodes may spontaneously rupture through the skin leaving a chronic draining sinus that is difficult to manage without complete excision.
Prolonged lymphadenopathy causing diagnostic delay — unresolved nodes over 6 weeks may prompt repeated investigations; unexplained delay in a node over 3 cm warrants biopsy to exclude lymphoma.

supportResources.name: CDC — Skin and Soft Tissue Infections
supportResources.description: CDC guidance for healthcare providers on MRSA skin and soft tissue infections including lymphadenitis management.

supportResources.name: AAP Red Book Online — Lymphadenitis
supportResources.description: American Academy of Pediatrics guidance on diagnosis and antibiotic selection for pediatric lymphadenitis and lymphadenopathy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lymphoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lymphoma typically presents as painless, rubbery, non-erythematous, often bilateral lymph node enlargement without fever or primary infection focus; nodes are non-tender and do not fluctuate. Acute lymphadenitis is painful, warm, erythematous, and arises abruptly in relation to an infection. Lymphoma nodes persist beyond 6 weeks and require biopsy for diagnosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Erysipelas
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Erysipelas is a sharply demarcated bright-red superficial dermis and lymphatic skin infection, not lymph-node swelling. It spreads rapidly across the skin surface and produces a palpably raised edge. Lymphadenitis is a focal deep swelling centered on the node, often with identifiable fluctuance, rather than a spreading cutaneous plate.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tuberculosis (scrofula)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tuberculous cervical lymphadenitis (scrofula) presents as painless, slowly enlarging, matted, non-tender nodes, typically bilateral or in the posterior triangle, often with sinus tract formation. Constitutional symptoms — night sweats, weight loss, prolonged low-grade fever — are common. QuantiFERON-TB test, chest X-ray, and FNAC with acid-fast smear and PCR distinguish TB from acute bacterial lymphadenitis.

confusedWithConditions.name: HIV/AIDS-related lymphadenopathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: HIV-related persistent generalized lymphadenopathy involves bilateral, symmetric, non-tender nodes at multiple sites simultaneously in early infection, without a primary focus. HIV test, CD4 count, and HIV RNA distinguish this from the unilateral, tender, site-specific presentation of acute bacterial lymphadenitis.

relatedConditions.name: Acute lymphangitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Acute lymphangitis involves inflammation of lymphatic vessels proximal to a skin infection and frequently co-occurs with or precedes lymphadenitis in the draining node.

relatedConditions.name: Impetigo
relatedConditions.relevance: A common primary skin infection by S. aureus and group A strep that drains to regional lymph nodes, causing lymphadenitis in a significant proportion of untreated or severely infected children.

relatedConditions.name: Tuberculosis
relatedConditions.relevance: TB scrofula is the most common extrapulmonary form of tuberculosis and presents as cervical lymphadenitis; both conditions must be distinguished in patients from high-burden regions.

relatedConditions.name: HIV/AIDS
relatedConditions.relevance: HIV both causes lymphadenopathy directly in primary infection and predisposes to atypical organisms causing lymphadenitis in patients with CD4 counts below 200 cells/µL.

faqs.question: What is acute lymphadenitis?
faqs.answer: Acute lymphadenitis is a rapid, painful swelling of one or more lymph nodes caused by a bacterial or viral infection draining from a nearby skin wound, dental abscess, throat infection, or other source. The node becomes tender, red, warm, and sometimes fills with pus.

faqs.question: What causes acute lymphadenitis?
faqs.answer: Staphylococcus aureus and group A Streptococcus together cause 65-80% of bacterial cases. Viral causes include Epstein-Barr virus and adenovirus. Cat-scratch disease (Bartonella henselae) and dental infections are other common triggers. The organism travels from the primary infection site through lymphatic vessels to the node.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of acute lymphadenitis?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include rapid swelling and tenderness of a lymph node, overlying skin redness and warmth, fever, and sometimes fluctuance (feeling of fluid under the skin) if an abscess forms. The node may become matted and difficult to separate from adjacent nodes in severe cases.

faqs.question: How is acute lymphadenitis treated?
faqs.answer: Non-suppurative cases are treated with oral antibiotics targeting Staphylococcus and Streptococcus — cefalexin or amoxicillin-clavulanate for 10-14 days. If CA-MRSA is suspected, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or clindamycin is used. Abscesses require incision and drainage plus antibiotics.

faqs.question: When does lymphadenitis need surgery?
faqs.answer: Surgery or needle aspiration is needed when an abscess forms — signaled by fluctuance, thinning of overlying skin, and failure to improve on oral antibiotics. Deep cervical abscesses require surgical drainage under general anesthetic to prevent airway compromise or spread to the mediastinum.

faqs.question: How long does acute lymphadenitis last?
faqs.answer: With appropriate antibiotic treatment, fever resolves within 48-72 hours and lymph node tenderness improves within 5-7 days. Full resolution of node swelling takes 2-4 weeks. A small fibrous nodule may persist for several months after the infection clears.

faqs.question: Is lymphadenitis the same as lymphadenopathy?
faqs.answer: Lymphadenitis specifically means inflamed lymph nodes with redness, tenderness, and fever — typically from an infection. Lymphadenopathy is a broader term for any lymph node enlargement, including non-infectious causes like lymphoma, autoimmune disease, or drug reactions.

faqs.question: Can lymphadenitis be dangerous?
faqs.answer: Most cases resolve safely with antibiotics. Serious complications occur in a minority: bacteremia and sepsis, deep-space neck infection with airway risk, or Lemierre syndrome (jugular vein thrombosis). Cervical lymphadenitis with fever above 39°C and neck stiffness requires emergency evaluation.

faqs.question: Can lymphadenitis be caused by MRSA?
faqs.answer: Yes. Community-acquired methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (CA-MRSA) is an increasingly common cause of purulent lymphadenitis, particularly in children. It presents as rapidly fluctuant abscesses and does not respond to standard beta-lactam antibiotics. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or clindamycin is used empirically in high-prevalence areas.

faqs.question: How do doctors distinguish lymphadenitis from lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Lymphadenitis is painful, warm, erythematous, and associated with a source infection. Lymphoma nodes are typically painless, rubbery, non-tender, and persist beyond 6 weeks without improvement. Biopsy — via fine-needle aspiration or core needle — is required for any node that does not resolve within 4-6 weeks of antibiotics.

faqs.question: What antibiotics treat lymphadenitis?
faqs.answer: Cefalexin, amoxicillin-clavulanate, or clindamycin are first-line oral antibiotics for non-MRSA cases. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is preferred when CA-MRSA is likely. Intravenous vancomycin is used for systemic sepsis. The course typically lasts 10-14 days.

faqs.question: Can viral infections cause lymphadenitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Viral infections — especially Epstein-Barr virus (infectious mononucleosis), CMV, and adenovirus — commonly cause bilateral cervical lymph node enlargement. Viral lymphadenitis is usually less tender than bacterial cases, bilateral, and resolves without antibiotics over 2-6 weeks.

faqs.question: Why do children get lymphadenitis more than adults?
faqs.answer: Children under 10 encounter group A Streptococcus and S. aureus through tonsillitis, otitis media, and skin infections more frequently than adults. Their immune systems are less primed, and lymph nodes react more vigorously to first exposures. Cervical lymphadenitis is among the most common bacterial infections requiring surgery in children.

faqs.question: Is cat-scratch disease a cause of lymphadenitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cat-scratch disease, caused by Bartonella henselae transmitted via kitten scratches or bites, causes regional lymphadenitis — most often axillary or cervical — appearing 1-3 weeks after a scratch. A papule or pustule marks the inoculation site. Diagnosis is confirmed by Bartonella serology. Most cases in healthy people resolve without treatment over 2-4 months.

faqs.question: What is the difference between lymphadenitis and lymphangitis?
faqs.answer: Lymphangitis is inflammation of lymphatic vessels, appearing as red streaks tracking from a skin infection toward a lymph node. Lymphadenitis is the resulting inflammation of the draining node itself. Both often occur together as the infection ascends the lymphatic system from a primary wound or skin infection site.
